title: Revealed Privacy Preferences: Are Privacy Choices Rational?
abstract: We investigate the extent to which trade-offs involving the sharing of personal information exhibit consistency with an underlying rational preference for privacy. In an experiment, people engage in trade-offs across two domains of personal information sharing, allowing us to classify whether their choices satisfy the generalized axiom of revealed preference. They also subsequently price the sharing of their personal information. Sixty-three percent of subjects act consistently with a rational preference ordering when allocating privacy levels, a level comparable to that observed in other domains. Individuals who are inconsistent when engaging in simple privacy trade-offs exhibit substantially more costly preference reversals when pricing personal information sharing. Only one-third of subjects exhibit consistency across both the selection of personal information to share and the pricing of such information sharing. Our results imply that monetizing personal information can have distinct welfare consequences for people with different degrees of rationality in their underlying ability to make sensible trade-offs involving personal information. We also provide evidence that preferences elicited over choices in our experiment correlate with real-world privacy behaviors.

author.Yi-Shan Lee: CUHK Business School, Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shatin, Hong Kong
author.Roberto A. Weber: Department of Economics, University of Zurich, 8001 Zurich, Switzerland

article.1. Introduction: Decisions involving privacy play a critical role in the information economy, influencing the effectiveness of online marketing (Goldfarb and Tucker 2011), crowdfunding (Burtch et al. 2015), market concentration in the technology sector (Peukert et al. 2022), and technology adoption in health (Miller and Tucker 2009,2018). How households and consumers manage the sharing of their private information is relevant for managers and firms across a variety of industries, including social media and e-commerce. Individuals’ ability to engage in sensible trade-offs involving their personal information is also critical for the design of policies that weigh potential benefits from information sharing against concerns about protecting privacy. If individuals cannot behave rationally when sharing their personal data, such violations of rationality can justify the need for privacy regulations.1 Many aspects of decisions involving personal information sharing are likely to contribute to biased and inconsistent behavior—including complexity, incomplete information and ambiguity, and intertemporal trade-offs. However, these aspects can also yield irrational and biased behavior in other domains unrelated to information sharing. An important question is whether engaging in trade-offs and pricing over one’s personal information per se creates challenges for rational individual behavior even when other common sources of irrationality are eliminated. People have exchanged goods and services for centuries, but the widespread exchange and sharing of personal data are relatively novel activities, raising questions about whether people are as capable of sensibly managing trade-offs in the domain of information sharing as they are in other contexts. Basic notions of economic rationality provide a means for objectively measuring whether individuals’ choices are consistent with models based on utility maximization. Such measures identify the extent to which choices satisfy the necessary properties to be represented by a complete and transitive preference ordering. Whereas such tests involve trade-offs over simple combinations of known outcomes, usually over two dimensions, they provide valuable information on whether individuals handle simple trade-offs in a consistent manner, and they have been used to measure rationality across a wide variety of domains (Cox 1997,Sippel 1997,Mattei 2000,Harbaugh et al. 2001,Andreoni and Miller 2002,Choi et al. 2007,Fisman et al. 2007,Choi et al. 2014).2In the context of privacy and information sharing, these simple tests can be useful for identifying whether the domain of personal information sharing is one whose basic nature makes it inherently challenging for rational and consistent behavior. They also inform the extent to which preferences over simple privacy decisions can be modeled by utility functions, which is essential for analyzing privacy trade-offs using economic models. In this study, we investigate the degree to which privacy choices are consistent with an underlying rational preference ordering by applying a “revealed privacy preference” approach to two kinds of privacy decisions: (i) sharing different kinds of personal information, and (ii) trade-offs between privacy control and money. Both kinds of decisions are valuable for understanding whether people can engage in simple trade-offs involving the sharing of their personal information. The first kind of choice sheds light on how people decide privacy levels for different forms of personal information and whether they act as if they are maximizing a coherent set of preferences that can be characterized by a utility function. The second kind of choice quantifies the cost of decision errors when people must consider trading off privacy for money. The measurement of simple notions of rationality in these two kinds of privacy decisions provides insights into the degree to which individuals face challenges in managing even simple trade-offs in the domain of personal information sharing.3The frequency of violations of rationality in these two kinds of privacy decisions also provides insights into the kind of privacy trade-offs that individuals find particularly challenging and that may require policy attention. In an exploratory part of our analysis, we also study the connection between individuals’ privacy choices in the two stylized laboratory contexts given and their privacy behavior outside the laboratory. Using a novel approach to elicit incentivized measures of individuals’ personal information sharing in various real-world contexts, we investigate the extent to which these real-life privacy decisions relate to the measures of rationality and preferences elicited in our laboratory tasks. We thus provide suggestive evidence on the degree to which measures of rationality and preferences constructed by observing simple trade-offs are relevant for understanding real-world privacy behavior. An important property of our study is that the use of subjects’ data and the consequences of personal information sharing are clear and understandable. Hence, this setup presents a useful starting point for understanding the rationality of privacy preferences in a setting where the trade-offs are simple and explicitly specified, with known consequences. Real-world settings involving privacy and information sharing are typically substantially more complex, involving, for example, ambiguous consequences and intertemporal trade-offs. If subjects manage their personal data sensibly in the contexts we study, this does not guarantee that they will do so in the more complex choices they encounter outside the laboratory. However, if individuals struggle to act consistently even in the simple information-sharing tasks with limited complexity and known consequences that we study, this suggests that they will likely have an even harder time managing more complex privacy trade-offs in the real world. In this sense, the level of rationality we observe in this study is something of an upper bound on the extent to which people can rationally manage their privacy. Moreover, the level of observed consistency in the simple trade-offs with known consequences that we study here can be compared with the level of consistency observed in other domains, including ones involving purely monetary consequences. This feature allows us to shed light on whether the domain of personal information sharing poses unique challenges to rational decision making beyond those that result from features of the choice contexts in which privacy choices are typically made. In the first part of this study, we begin by testing whether individuals engage in trade-offs involving the sharing of different personal data items in a systematic manner consistent with a stable underlying preference. At the core of rational privacy behavior lies the deliberate concealment or revelation of personal information (Posner 1981), which we test using a simple choice setting. Specifically, we ask individuals to choose their most preferred privacy level, measured by the number of people who will view two kinds of “reports” containing different forms of personal information: a measure of the subject’s intelligence and the subject’s body fat composition. These forms of personal information reflect the kinds of individual qualities, such as grades and health, that are relevant for labor and insurance markets and are frequently at the center of privacy debates. Although viewers only see the reports on a computer display individually and any reproduction or transmission of the personal information would not be possible, this design enables us to investigate how individuals manage privacy trade-offs when the uses of personal information are clearly defined and easily understood. We present these choices in a sequence of decision problems that vary in the relative “prices” of revealing the two kinds of information. Variation in the budget sets and the corresponding relative prices across decisions in this part provide a stringent test of whether choices satisfy the properties of utility maximization.4 More precisely, in part 1 of our study, we first classify people according to their privacy attitudes over these personal information domains and then evaluate the rationality of individual privacy choices by their consistency with the general axiom of revealed preference (GARP). We find that most (68%) of our subjects value the privacy of both information items as goods, and more than 72% of subjects of this private type make choices that are entirely consistent with the maximization of an underlying convex preference. The privacy attitudes of other people are public (10%), meaning they value sharing both kinds of information, or item dependent (22%). Despite the heterogeneity of privacy attitudes, we find more than 63% of individuals in our pooled sample share and conceal their personal data in a perfectly consistent manner. Hence, about two-thirds of our subjects’ privacy preferences involving simple trade-offs across different information domains can be represented as a well-behaved preference ordering consistent with utility maximization. This proportion of subjects acting consistently with well-behaved privacy preferences is comparable to the proportion of subjects acting consistently with well-behaved risk preferences with the analogous budget sets, which we elicit in a follow-up study. In addition, this proportion is comparable to those in several previous experimental studies testing consistency with GARP when making other types of trade-offs (Cox 1997,Sippel 1997,Mattei 2000,Harbaugh et al. 2001,Andreoni and Miller 2002,Choi et al. 2007,Fisman et al. 2007,Choi et al. 2014). These comparisons show that people manage trade-offs involving their personal information as well as in other economic choice contexts—at least in simple choices involving complete information, limited complexity, and direct and well-understood consequences—suggesting that trade-offs involving privacy in different information domains are not inherently more challenging for rationality and consistency than other kinds of economic trade-offs. In the second part of this study, we utilize the revealed privacy preferences from part 1 to test the extent to which people leave money on the table when privacy decisions involve trade-offs between privacy control and money. This provides insights into the efficiency and implications of tackling privacy issues by employing market mechanisms involving monetizing personal information. We compare the willingness to accept (WTA) for the revelation of bundles of information, including some bundles directly selected by subjects from the budget sets in the first part of the experiment and others unchosen from the same sets. A preference reversal in this context implies that subjects require a lower price for selling their information, as specified in a bundle,X, than for an alternative bundle that their choices in part 1 reveal as directly preferred toX. Interestingly, 47% of the subjects we classified as rational in part 1 exhibit such reversals, whereas 65% of those classified as irrational do so. We also quantify the degree of utility loss from mistakes people make when mapping their revealed choice preferences into monetary valuations, which we refer to as “monetary-value noise” (MVN). People whose privacy preference in part 1 violate GARP exhibit significantly larger average MVN—260% larger than the average MVN of individuals we classify as rational in part 1. These results suggest that moving to more complex choices involving the sharing of personal information—such as pricing one’s private information—may create additional impediments for rational behavior. Moreover, those individuals who are less capable of managing trade-offs involving the sharing of different kinds of personal information sensibly—the irrational types from part 1—may be more prone to costly errors in more complex settings involving privacy. Importantly, only 33% of subjects make consistent privacy choices across both parts 1 and 2 of our study. Thus, few individuals can engage in sensible trade-offs involving different dimensions of personal information and price bundles of their personal information in a manner consistent with their revealed preferences. This result is particularly striking given the simplicity of the choices presented to subjects in our experiment—it implies that engaging in rational information-sharing behavior may be more challenging in even more complex settings. Moreover, note that because privacy choices in this study simplify the real-life complexities of data use, even people who exhibit perfect valuation consistency through parts 1 and 2 may still be susceptible to biases when making privacy-related decisions in more complex settings (e.g.,Smith et al. 2011,Adjerid et al. 2018,Xu and Zhang 2022,Tomaino et al. 2023). Hence, our findings also raise concerns about the degree to which people are likely to sensibly manage the sharing of their personal information in complex trade-offs involving monetary or other material benefits. It is also important to note the heterogeneity in rationality we observe in parts 1 and 2 of our study and the implications of such heterogeneity for individuals’ ability to engage in rational information sharing. First, 37% of our subjects’ information-sharing choices cannot be reconciled with an underlying utility function incorporating privacy concerns. Thus, policy interventions attempting to shift behavior based on models of utility maximization may have limited effectiveness in influencing information sharing of such individuals. Second, even among people who satisfy GARP—and therefore may have a consistent underlying preference ordering—47% (a percentage conditional on satisfying GARP) exhibit inconsistency when asked to provide monetary valuations for their private information. Thus, even among people who might be classified as “rational” using a standard economic benchmark, we observe substantial inconsistency that suggests additional policy interventions may be particularly helpful when privacy choices involve monetary trade-offs. Our revealed preference approach to measuring privacy preferences is also validated by other patterns in our data. For example, individuals whose choices over simple bundles in part 1 of our study indicate a preference for privacy have a significantly higher mean WTA for sharing their data in part 2 than individuals whose part 1 choices indicate less concern for privacy. Thus, monetary valuations such as WTA seem to tell us something meaningful about privacy preferences, at least in comparisons between different groups of people. This result corroborates earlier evidence of a correlation between self-reported privacy attitudes and the valuation of personal data (Kokolakis 2017), but using incentivized measures of privacy attitudes with real consequences in the sharing of personal information. It also provides evidence on the extent to which monetary valuations such as WTA, extensively used in measuring privacy valuations, capture privacy attitudes measured in choices not involving money. The third part of this study involves an exploratory investigation into the relationship between our primary privacy measures in the laboratory and other measures of privacy preferences and behavior outside the laboratory. Because the scale of collection and sharing of personal data has increased significantly in the information age, it is essential to understand the extent to which this creates economic value that individuals can manage in a rational and beneficial manner. Thus, we explore whether individuals we classify differently based on their privacy orientations and rationality in part 1 of our experiment exhibit different real-world behavioral patterns when managing their personal information. We investigate this link between the laboratory and the field by eliciting information on subjects’ daily-life sharing of personal details using incentivized questions. Specifically, we elicit concrete measures of how much information subjects share online and with firms and provide them with incentives for truthfully revealing instances of information sharing. We then test whether individuals with different privacy attitudes and degrees of rationality behave differently when sharing their personal data. Although these exploratory results must be interpreted cautiously, we find evidence of a relationship between our laboratory privacy measures and some individual privacy behavior in the field. Specifically, factor analysis identifies two primary domains of daily-life information sharing in our data: social networking use (SN) and the exchange of personal information for money or services (EMS). We find that SN correlates strongly with privacy attitudes but not with rationality, whereas EMS has a modest correlation with rationality. We cautiously interpret these correlational results as suggesting that individuals’ privacy preferences have some underlying stability and that the relationship between such preferences and behavior may be domain specific. Finally, we also use our laboratory measures of privacy preferences to test the validity of a widely used self-reported measure of privacy attitudes. We find that private types who prefer to show their personal information to fewer viewers in the experiment also report greater concern for privacy in general. Thus, we provide further validation for this question as a measure of privacy preferences. Broadly, our study contributes to advancing the measurement of privacy preferences and understanding the extent to which individuals can engage in rational trade-offs when making decisions involving the use of their personal information. Employing GARP as a necessary condition for rationality, we find significant heterogeneity in how rational people can be when managing their privacy, even in the very simple contexts we study. Importantly, however, the degree to which people violate GARP in the domain of information sharing is not substantially different than comparable violations in other domains not involving privacy. We also find that people who fail to satisfy this necessary condition for rationality suffer substantial monetary losses when trading off their privacy control for money. Finally, simple laboratory choices measuring privacy preferences have some predictive value for real-world privacy behaviors and general privacy attitudes. Our results also suggest that careful consideration should be given to the heterogeneity of rationality and its monetary consequences when designing privacy policies and that simple stylized choices can be used to understand important aspects of how people approach managing and sharing their personal information. The remainder of the article is structured as follows.Section 2reviews related literature.Section 3describes the experimental design.Section 4reports results of the three parts of our study:Section 4.1studies the rationality of privacy choices when deciding between bundles of information sharing,Section 4.2presents the consistency between these privacy choices and their monetary equivalents, andSection 4.3further analyzes the consistency between laboratory privacy measures and privacy behaviors in the field and general privacy attitudes.Section 5concludes.
article.2. Related Literature: Our project is motivated by the question of whether individuals rationally manage decisions involving the sharing of their personal information. This question is at the core of debates regarding the need for privacy protection policies. Pioneered by Chicago School scholars George Stigler (Stigler 1961,1962,1980) and Richard Posner (Posner 1978,1981), the antiregulation perspective highlights that privacy protection is redistributive and creates market inefficiency. Many subsequent economic studies analyzing optimal policy and welfare in markets involving privacy and information sharing assume rational behavior and utility maximization and largely ignore the question of whether individuals manage personal data sharing rationally. Instead, the focus is typically on other aspects of markets for privacy that affect welfare, such as how information sharing impacts opportunities for price discrimination (Ichihashi 2020), potential externalities from information sharing (Choi et al. 2019,Acemoglu et al. 2022), and the role of varying competition in markets for information (Casadesus-Masanell and Hervas-Drane 2015,Bergemann and Bonatti 2019). Among the few exceptions,Taylor (2004)andVillas-Boas (2004)highlight individuals’ ability to control and share personal data rationally and strategically as potentially critical factors for the welfare impacts of regulated and unregulated markets for personal information. However, from the early stages in the discussion of policy in the context of information sharing, the rationality assumptions underlying the Chicago privacy models and their welfare implications were concurrently questioned byHirshleifer (1971,1980) and later by many other scholars. Considerable evidence—a large part of it from psychology, law, and information sciences—questions the assumption that individuals rationally manage the sharing of their personal information.Acquisti et al. (2015),Kokolakis (2017), andAcquisti et al. (2020)review empirical studies on the influence of behavioral and psychological phenomena on privacy choices. For example, studies of self-reported real-world information sharing indicate that people fail to properly balance the immediate benefits of sharing information with potential long-term consequences (Acquisti and Grossklags 2005) and that their privacy behavior is influenced by framing and the choice context (Acquisti et al. 2013,Benndorf and Normann 2017) and by default effects (Acquisti and Gross 2006,Stutzman et al. 2012). Many of the apparent biases and inconsistencies documented in these studies, however, may not be unique to individuals’ difficulty in managing the sharing of their personal information but may also arise more generally in other settings containing features such as complexity, asymmetric information, and intertemporal trade-offs (Acquisti 2004). For example, the influence of defaults and framing effects on privacy behavior may be rationalized by the broader phenomenon of context-dependent preferences (Kalai et al. 2002). Thus, it is an open question whether evidence of widespread irrationality in privacy behavior results from individuals’ inherent difficulty in managing personal information sharing per se or because privacy choices regularly occur in settings that contain features that yield irrational behavior. By studying the rationality in individuals’ privacy behavior in stylized contexts involving simple trade-offs with known consequences, we can both identify the degree of rationality in the sharing of personal information absent many features that make such decisions challenging in real-world contexts and compare the degree of rational behavior to analogous benchmarks in contexts not involving privacy. Inspired by the importance of rationality in the literature on the privacy protection debate, this project aims to fill important gaps in the literature by being the first to directly test individuals’ rationality when making two essential privacy trade-offs: between the sharing of different kinds of personal information and between privacy control and monetary payoffs. Our study involves a revealed privacy preference approach in which we study individuals’ choices over the sharing of actual bundles of their personal information as a way of understanding how they confront decisions involving privacy (Huberman et al. 2005,Benndorf and Normann 2017,Schudy and Utikal 2017). The key attribute of a choice in our study is the number of other individuals who observe one’s private information.Schudy and Utikal (2017)validate that privacy, defined according to the number of strangers receiving personal information, is valued as an economic good. In particular, individuals’ willingness to share their personal data weakly decreases in the number of recipients and varies across information items. This work provides a foundation for our first trade-off task, in which we ask whether people trade off privacy levels across different personal information domains in asystematicway consistent with an underlying preference assigning (dis)utility to the sharing of personal information. Such trade-offs are relevant for privacy behavior—for example, social networking site users already report that if they could grant access to limited viewers, they would have shared approximately half the unshared content (Sleeper et al. 2013). Findings like those earlier suggest that people care about the degree to which others have access to their personal information and that such concerns for privacy are heterogeneous across individuals. Our first trade-off task tests whether individuals’ preferred privacy levels across information items vary systematically in response to implicit “prices” and thus exhibit rationally in an important basic economic sense (Dembo et al. 2021). On the other hand, the literature on trade-offs between privacy control and monetary benefits shows that the monetary value of privacy control is not only heterogeneous across individuals but is also sensitive to the choice context. InSchudy and Utikal (2017), the monetary value of privacy across individuals exhibits a high degree of heterogeneity: around 12% of participants always share personal information to earn money, whereas about 20% of participants never share, and the decisions of the rest of participants depend on monetary payoffs. However, the monetary value of privacy control not only varies individually but also varies depending on the elicitation method (Benndorf and Normann 2017), the endowment and the order of privacy choices (Acquisti et al. 2013), and the salience of privacy control (Tsai et al. 2011,Beresford et al. 2012). Surprisingly, even as monetary value varies, the share of individuals sharing a particular item can remain relatively stable (Benndorf and Normann 2017). This phenomenon raises the possibility that people, in principle, know whether they want to share a particular information item but are unsure how to map this decision into monetary valuations. Although many studies investigate how people value various types of privacy control in different scenarios and using various methodologies (for a detailed survey, seeKokolakis 2017), taking monetary valuation as a good measure of privacy preferences requires an auxiliary assumption—specifically, that people can sensibly translate their privacy preferences into monetary values—that has yet to be thoroughly tested. The validation of this assumption is essential for the legitimacy of utilizing market mechanisms to allocate privacy, as proposed in a substantial literature on privacy protection (Laudon 1996,Samuelson 2000,Schwartz 2004,Varian 2009,Ghosh and Roth 2015). Several studies focusing on the issue of inconsistencies in privacy preferences have shown that despite individuals reporting being concerned with privacy, they appear to have surprisingly low thresholds for sharing specific personal information. For example,Spiekermann et al. (2001)use a simulated shopping experience to document that even individuals who express high levels of privacy concern seem to voluntarily share private information, such as their home address, for limited benefits.Beresford et al. (2012)similarly find insensitivity to the collection of individuals’ personal information in a field experiment on online shopping, even in a sample that reports assigning high importance to personal privacy. This seeming inconsistency is often referred to as “the privacy paradox,” a phenomenon reviewed extensively inKokolakis (2017). Notably, the privacy attitude at the core of this paradox is typically measured through self-reported privacy concerns instead of incentivized choices. Hence, it is still an open question whether the privacy dichotomy reflects an inconsistency between privacy attitudes and behavior or an inconsistency between self-reported privacy concerns and consequential privacy behavior. Our study contributes by eliciting incentivized measures of privacy attitudes and showing that these correlate both with self-reported general privacy attitudes and with privacy behavior, at least in the simple contexts we study. Recent work has argued that apparent inconsistencies and biases in how individuals manage the sharing of their personal information require policies aimed at protecting consumers by facilitating their ability to keep personal information private (Acquisti et al. 2015,2017). However, there is also evidence that providing individuals with disclosures regarding the use of their personal information and tools to facilitate control over such information can, paradoxically, lead to greater information sharing (Brandimarte et al. 2013,Tucker 2014). This may be because of a false sense of security provided by such interventions.5These findings suggest that policy approaches aimed at ensuring individuals’ ability to make decisions in a state of informed consent may fail to provide people “meaningful control over their data” (Solove 2013, p. 1880). Although our study does not specifically examine whether informed consent improves or worsens decision making, it demonstrates that in simple decisions made with clear and easily understood consequences regarding the sharing of personal information, choices involving privacy do not exhibit greater irrationality than decisions involving purely monetary consequences.
article.3. The Experiment: 3.1. Design Overview The central part of our experiment consists of an assessment process to generate personal information and three stages in which subjects make decisions. A detailed timeline of the experiment is shown inTable 1. We first provide a broad description of the design and then explain each stage in detail. Table 1.Experimental Procedures Table 1.Experimental Procedures During the assessment process, each subject generates two different forms of private personal information: a body fat composition score and an intelligence score. These two scores reflect the kinds of individual qualities—such as grades and health—that are frequently at the center of privacy debates and are relevant in labor markets and insurance contexts. Subjects are told that this information may subsequently be used to generate two hard copy “assessment reports,” each including a subject’s name, signature, picture, and one of the two pieces of personal information mentioned. Stages 1 and 2 involve making decisions that may lead to subjects forfeiting their control over the personal information in these two reports in exchange for monetary rewards. The choices in these stages involve “report viewing combinations” of a subject’s personal information. Each combination specifies a given number of strangers—fellow students from the same general university population—who will view their body composition score and another given number of (different) strangers who will view their intelligence score. As a result of their choices in stages 1 and 2, a particular bundle may be implemented, meaning that the corresponding reports are shown to the specified number of individuals. An important feature of our design is that we operationalize variation in “privacy” through the number of people who view one’s personal information. This represents a natural and meaningful, yet simple, starting point for investigating preferences over the sharing of information in a controlled manner. It is motivated by earlier work demonstrating that individuals care about the number of viewers who observe their personal information (Schudy and Utikal 2017). Moreover, the set and number of people who can view information are common metrics and settings in online information sharing. Social media platforms often provide some control over who can view one’s content and may reward users, monetarily or psychologically, for larger numbers of viewers. Privacy settings on many General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR)–compliant websites allow users to indicate the sets of companies that can use cookies to access one’s private information. We also deliberately chose a population of viewers—fellow students in the same city—about whom our student participants might care about privacy with respect to these types of information. Whereas, admittedly, the number of anonymous viewers of two specific informational items is distinct from richer forms of viewership and use of one’s private data in most consequential settings, they share the basic structure of individuals deciding on the extent of sharing of their personal information. Hence, variation in the number of fellow student viewers of one’s body fat composition and intelligence, next to an individual’s portrait, represents a natural, controlled, and easily understood way to model more complex forms of data sharing and privacy. This simple two-dimensional informational context also allows us to test for consistency with the general axiom of revealed preference. In stage 1, subjects are confronted with 20 budget sets, each presenting several possible bundles from which a subject chooses one. Subjects thus select 20 possible bundles, thereby indicating their preferred use of their personal information given the feasible budgets. At the end of stage 1, the computer selects another 20 possible uses. Then, in stage 2, subjects decide, for each of the 40 possible uses, for what range of prices they are willing to sell their personal information so that it is used in the described manner. Choices in these two stages determine how the personal information in the two assessment reports is used and also the main portion of each subject’s final payment. Finally, in stage 3, subjects answer an incentivized questionnaire about their daily-life personal information sharing behavior. Details of the procedures for each stage are described in the next section. The experiment lasted two hours, and subjects received a participation fee of CHF 40 (Swiss francs, with CHF 1 ≈ USD 1). However, they could add to this payment during the experiment. The final payments consisted of anonymous bonus payments for intelligence tests that ranged from CHF 0 to CHF 6 and main payments, including the participation fee, that ranged from CHF 40 to CHF 95.5.6 3.2. Stage 0: Assessment Process At the beginning of the experiment, subjects provided three pieces of personal information: a profile picture taken by the experimenter, a measure of body fat composition, and an intelligence score.7Subjects completed this assessment privately, and their scores were only observed by one experimenter, who performed no other function in the session than administering the assessment and generating a single written record of the scores. This experimenter had no access to subjects’ names or any other personally identifying information. All information was recorded in hard copies by the experimenter. Then, the subject received hard copies of two “assessment reports” containing these data in closed envelopes. The reports contained the following information: a subject’s portrait photograph and either the subject’s body fat or the subject’s intelligence. At this point, the subject’s name was not on the report, though there was a blank space where this information could be recorded. Meanwhile, subjects were aware that they could make choices in later stages of the experiment for their assessment reports to be shown to “viewers”—randomly recruited strangers from the University of Zurich (UZH) and the Swiss Federal Institute of Technology (ETH). This design mimics sharing information online with a sample of students in one’s university community (as often happens in social media), while making clear and holding constant the viewers’ identity. The viewers were subsequently recruited from public places at the two universities to view a series of assessment reports in exchange for a small payment. Subjects were also informed that, if their assessment reports would be shown to viewers, viewers would see the reports on a computer display individually, but the reproduction or transmission of the personal information would not be possible. This design controls subjects’ beliefs about how their personal information will be used and enables us to investigate how they manage privacy trade-offs when the consequences of sharing personal information are clearly defined. 3.3. Stage 1: Specifying Uses of Personal Information In this stage, each subject chose 20 possible uses for their assessment reports from feasible sets by indicating their most preferred “report-viewing combination” in each of 20 decision problems. A combination (x, y) refers to some number,x, of people who will view the body composition report and another number,y, of people who will view the intelligence report. Subjects were aware that the computer would then choose another 20 combinations and that these combined 40 report-viewing combinations would subsequently be the only possible implemented uses of their reports after stage 1. Thus, by selecting a combination from the feasible set in a decision problem, a subject made it more likely that this combination would subsequently be implemented. This point was explained to subjects. In the first four decision problems of stage 1 (stage 1A), subjects created a combination of viewers by choosing the number of viewers for each assessment report independently without making trade-offs between the privacy levels of the two assessment reports. Two of these questions were single-dimensional in the sense that subjects chose freely from the range [0,125] for one report, and the number of viewers of the other report was fixed at zero. These two questions were used to familiarize subjects with the interface. The choice set of the other two questions in Stage 1A was two-dimensional, as shown by the two shaded squares inFigure 1(a). One question has a range [0,80] for both reports, and the other has a range of [45,125] for both reports. On these two questions, subjects could choose any point in the square choice set in increments of five viewers. Thus, these choices allow us to observe what direction a subject prefers in a two-dimensional space of possible viewing combinations. We later use the decisions in these two-dimensional questions to classify subjects into different privacy attitudes: private (a preference for fewer viewers on both dimensions), public (more viewers on both dimensions) or item-dependent (more viewers on one dimension and fewer on the other). In the remaining 16 decision problems of stage 1 (stage 1B), subjects made trade-offs between privacy levels for the two assessment reports. In each problem, subjects chose a combination consisting of some number of viewers for each report, requiring trade-offs between the viewers for the two reports at “prices” that were indicated in the decision problem. The full set of budgeted privacy levels is shown inFigure 1(b). In combination, these budget sets allow us to identify GARP violations for each possible kind of privacy attitude. Figure 2(a)shows an example of the interface used to represent the budget sets to subjects. In this example, a subject may explore all linear combinations on a budget line connecting two extreme combinations: combination A (100 viewers of the body composition report and zero viewers of the intelligence report) and combination B (zero viewers of the body composition report and 50 viewers of the intelligence report) by using a slider that controls both slider bars simultaneously. The implicit price for this budget line equals two body composition viewers for one (e.g., 36 viewers of the body composition report and 32 viewers of the intelligence report) on the budget line.8 At the end of stage 1, subjects had selected 20 combinations in total. This included two from one-dimensional problems, two from the independent two-dimensional privacy-attitude measures, and 16 from downward-sloping budget sets that allow us to identify GARP violations. 3.4. Stage 2: Reviewing and Selling the Use of Personal Information At the beginning of stage 2, the computer generated 16 additional combinations—one from each of the downward-sloping budget sets—for each of our subjects. Specifically, for each budget set inFigure 1(b), the computer randomly chose one of the two points that was exactly five units away from the combination chosen by a subject.9Thus, each of these 16 combinations represents a bundle that was not selected when a different bundle available in the same budget set was chosen. This procedure we use for selecting these combinations keeps the difference in the number of viewers between perturbed choices and chosen choices constant across subjects. In stage 2, subjects then traded off privacy control against monetary payoffs for 40 report-viewing combinations, including all 20 report-viewing combinations they had selected in stage 1: the 16 additional combinations selected by the computer as described earlier and four additional preset combinations that were the same for all participants: (20,20), (65,65), (0,65), and (65,0), represented by the four grey dots inFigure 1(b). These additional four combinations allow us to make direct comparisons across subjects’ valuations for a fixed set of bundles. Subjects first learned the range of possible prices (from 0 CHF to 40 CHF) from which the computer would offer them a randomly selected price for selling their personal information in each decision problem. For each possible use of their information (a combination of viewers for each report), they then decided the lowest acceptable price for which they were willing to sell their personal information and allow it to be viewed by people according to that report-viewing combination. Subjects always had the option to state a price of “more than 40 CHF” to indicate that they were unwilling to sell the personal information at any of the possible prices.Figure 2(b)shows an example of the interface in stage 2. At the end of the experiment, the computer would randomly select one of the 40 report-viewing combinations from stage 2 to be the one that could potentially be implemented and determine subjects’ earnings. Each combination could be selected with equal probability. The Becker-DeGroot-Marschak mechanism (Becker et al. 1964) was then employed to determine whether, for the randomly selected combination, either the subject received a randomly determined price between 0 and 40 CHF and the assessment reports were shown to the corresponding number of viewers or whether the subject received no extra payment and no assessment report would be viewed by strangers. That is, the computer randomly selected a random price,p, between CHF 0 and CHF 40 and one of the combinations presented to the subject in stage 2. Depending on whetherpwas higher than the indicated lowest acceptable price by the subject for that decision problem, two possible cases could then arise: Case 1: Ifpwas higher than or equal to the lowest acceptable price, the subject received the extra paymentpfor selling their personal information. The randomly drawn offer price was added to the payment, and the personal information was used in the manner specified in the report-viewing combination, meaning that each of the two reports would be shown to the specified number of viewers. Case 2: Ifpwas lower than the lowest acceptable price, the subject did not sell the personal information, and the randomly drawn offer price was not added to the payment for this experiment. In this case, the subject shredded all the personal data in the envelopes at the end of the experiment.10 3.5. Stage 3: Incentivized Questions on Daily Privacy Behavior In this stage, subjects answered incentivized questions about their daily-life privacy behaviors in various real-world contexts. Specifically, we collected measures of the amount of personal information shared with the public on Facebook, the number of active social media accounts, the number of store memberships or club programs in use, the settings of location services on the subject’s mobile phone and the number of friends on Facebook. For each of the topics, subjects earned a basic amount of 1 CHF for providing an answer to that question and a potential bonus of 0.5 CHF for each additional piece of verifiable information they provided. For instance, when a subject was asked to indicate whether he or she shared different information items on Facebook, the subject earned no additional money for not providing an answer or stating, “I don’t know.” But, for every information item for which subjects provided an answer indicating that they actually shared or did not share that item, they could earn an additional 0.5 CHF if their answers were randomly selected to be checked and were accurate. At the end of the experiment, each subject had a probability of 10% (implemented by rolling a 10-sided die) of having a random check in which the experimenter attempted to verify that subject’s answers. Ifallthe information provided in stage 3 was verified to be accurate, the subject received the potential bonus payments for providing accurate, verifiable information. If any part of a subject’s responses were found to be inaccurate, all of that subject’s payments from stage 3 (ranging from 5 to 20 CHF if answered honestly) were forfeited. Hence, a subject unsure about an answer was instructed that it was in that subject’s best interest to honestly report, “I don’t know.” Potential payoffs in each decision problem are shown to subjects instantaneously according to the chosen answers to make this incentive-compatible mechanism intuitive (see Online Appendix B for an example of the stage 3 interface). 3.6. Stage 4: Questionnaire Finally, in stage 4, subjects completed a questionnaire. This questionnaire included several demographic measures. Subjects were also asked their subjective satisfaction regarding their intelligence and body composition scores. Finally, we also elicited a single question measuring their general privacy attitude, using a variant of a question often used in the literature: “In general, how important do you think privacy is?” 3.7. Sample We recruited 225 subjects via the online registration tool h-root (Bock et al. 2014) from registered participants from the general student populations at the University of Zurich and the Swiss Federal Institute of Technology in Zurich. The experiment was conducted at the Laboratory for Behavioral and Experimental Economics at UZH on a rolling basis, with one subject at a time, to ensure subjects had full privacy control over their personal data even in the assessment stage. We implemented the experiment using the software z-Tree (Fischbacher 2007) and MATLAB. To minimize selection into the experiment by participants with differential attitudes toward privacy, subjects were not informed of the nature of the experiment before arriving. However, upon arrival, they were given a detailed description of the procedures of the experiment, which stressed the fact that they would always have full control over their personal information.11Subjects were also given the option to participate in an abbreviated experiment that offered a standard payment of CHF 20 and required no provision of sensitive personal information. This abbreviated experiment enables subjects to skip the assessment process, stages 1 and 2, and to only participate in stages 3 and 4. Compared with letting highly privacy-concerned subjects drop out directly, involving them in the abbreviated experiment provides us with some information on their preferences. Out of 225 subjects, seven subjects decided to participate only in the abbreviated experiment. There were also two subjects who decided not to participate at all after reading the written instructions. Thus, in total, 216 subjects finished the experiment.12 3.8. Follow-Up Experiment Using Monetary Choices In order to provide a benchmark comparison for the degree of rationality in our privacy experiment, we conducted a follow-up study using the same procedures and combination of budget sets in stage 1, but instead of making choices over levels of personal information sharing, participants made choices from budget sets over monetary losses. Specifically, we translated the possible “loss” in privacy (corresponding to more viewers of one’s personal information) to possible monetary losses by asking subjects to distribute losses between two accounts: a “blue” and a “red” account. Subjects were endowed with a starting balance of 200 points in both accounts and knew that the exchange rate was five points = CHF 1. They then distributed possible losses, in points, between the two accounts in each of the 20 (4 + 16) decision problems in stage 1 according to the budget sets inFigure 1. Following that, subjects went through stages 3 and 4.13In total, 94 individuals participated, recruited from the same population and in the same manner as for our privacy experiment. The instructions and detailed analysis for this follow-up study are available in Online Appendix F and Online Appendix G.
article.4. Results: 4.1. Rationality When Deciding Privacy Levels 4.1.1. Classification of Privacy Attitudes. Testing whether an individual’s privacy choices are in conformance with GARP requires knowledge of the individual’s attitude toward the sharing of the corresponding personal information. Privacy is recognized as a good for that information if one desires as few viewers as possible, and it is regarded as a bad if one wants as many viewers for that information as possible. An individual’s privacy attitude, therefore, determines in which direction one’s utility increases along with the privacy level, and GARP violations can be defined accordingly. We classify each of our subjects into one of four types of privacy attitudes: private (preferring less information sharing in both domains), public (preferring more information sharing in both domains), and two item-dependent types (keeping the body composition report private while showing the intelligence report (IQ+) and keeping the intelligence report private while showing the body composition report (BF+)). For this classification, we use subjects’ choices in the unrestricted two-dimensional privacy-attitude questions from stage 1A. We classify each of our subjects into the privacy attitude that generates predictions that are closest to the subject’s choices in the two-dimensional privacy attitude questions.14We adopt this classification in the remainder of this study. Subjects in our experiment exhibit heterogeneity of privacy attitudes.Figure 3(a)shows the percentage of subjects with different privacy attitudes, classified as described earlier, and the proportions of each type who exhibit consistency with GARP. In our experiment, 68% of subjects are private, and the remainder consists of around 14% of the IQ+ type, 8% of the BF+ type, and 10% of the public type. Thus, most of our subjects exhibit a preference for sharing less personal information in this context, which is consistent with our expectations. 4.1.2. Conformity with GARP. Our first result concerns whether individuals willfully conceal and reveal personal information in a manner that is consistent with the general axiom of revealed preference. Specifically, we apply GARP to our “report-viewing combination” data from stage 1 by comparing how the allocation of privacy levels across the two information items varies according to the relative price of privacy—the relative numbers of viewers that have to be exchanged across the different information domains. Our primary interest in stage 1 is to identify the degree to which individuals’ privacy choices are consistent with GARP. FollowingAfriat (1967), if our finite data set of report-viewing combinations generated by an individual is consistent with GARP, then the individual’s privacy-allocation choices can be rationalized by a utility function,U(x,y), withxandybeing the number of viewers for different information items.15To measure the degree of rationality for each type, we adapt the rationality test according to the different privacy attitudes—that is, the direction of preferences on the two dimensions of information sharing. Using these measures, we find, overall, more than 63% of subjects exhibit perfect consistency with GARP. In the following analysis, we classify these subjects as rational types and those who exhibit GARP violations as “irrational” types. As shown inFigure 3(a), the frequency of rational behavior is highest among those individuals that we separately classify as private types: more than 72% of the private subjects are rational. The rational percentages corresponding to the IQ+, BF+, and the public types are 50%, 35.29%, and 40.91%, respectively.16 Another way to look at the degree of rationality of privacy decisions is to measure how closely individual privacy choices adhere to GARP. It is possible that GARP violators are choosing in a manner that is very close to satisfying GARP, and the cost of the violations is minimal. In this case, we may not want to put too much weight on relatively inconsequential violations of economic rationality. We address this concern by adapting the critical cost efficiency index (CCEI) measure,e∗, inAfriat (1972)—a measure of how “close” GARP violations are to the rational standard in terms of the implied cost of a violation—to different types of privacy attitudes. Specifically, if a subject values privacy as a good (bad), then |1−e∗| indicates the loss (gain) for that subject when increasing (decreasing) the number of viewers in budget sets in order to accommodate all GARP violations for that individual. Figure 3(b)shows thee∗scores, arranged in increasing order, for individuals with different privacy attitudes. The averagee*scores for each privacy type are also displayed inTable 2. This average score is 1.0425 for private types and 0.9670 for public types, which implies that, on average, the privacy level needs to be reduced by about 4% to eliminate all GARP violations by a private subject and increased by about 3% to rationalize the choices of a public subject. Table 2.Comparison of Degree of Rationality Across Different Studies Table 2.Comparison of Degree of Rationality Across Different Studies a“Rational %” generally refers to the percentage of subjects exhibiting no GARP violations. bThe numbers in parentheses classify as rational any subject with CCEI scores above 0.95. This threshold is adapted to 1.05 for private types in our privacy study and for subjects in our follow-up study with monetary losses. cThis number is not reported inFisman et al. (2007)but, instead, in their working paper version (2005). The low number potentially reflects numerous small violations, perhaps because of the choice interface they employ. dFor our study with monetary losses, we only include subjects classified as valuing money (91 of 94). To provide some context for these numbers,Table 2compares the percentage of subjects acting consistently with GARP in this study with comparable statistics from several previous experimental studies testing consistency with GARP when making other types of trade-offs—between consumption goods (Cox 1997,Sippel 1997,Mattei 2000,Harbaugh et al. 2001), monetary payoffs between oneself and another (Andreoni and Miller 2002,Fisman et al. 2007), and between two possible monetary payoffs for oneself (Choi et al. 2007,Choi et al. 2014). The table also reports the Bronars power, a measure of how likely the budget sets employed are to detect violations by an individual choosing entirely at random. The Bronars power in previous studies ranges from 0.243 to one, with recent studies using high power levels close to or equal to one. Our power for private types who value privacy as an economic good is sufficiently high (one) to be comparable to these studies. For public types, our Bronars power (0.9398) is slightly lower than in recent studies.17 Among private types, the proportion of rational subjects in our privacy study (ranging from 72.8 to 83.7%, depending on whether one uses the 0.95 CCEI threshold proposed byVarian 1991to allow for minor violations) is higher than that in most of the previous studies, except inAndreoni and Miller (2002),18and the percentage of rational public subjects ranges from 40.9% to 90.9%, which is similar to the range observed in other studies (36.75% to 89.8%) and is higher than most recent studies when using the 0.95 CCEI threshold. Hence, overall, the proportion of individuals who allocate privacy levels rationally in our study does not seem to differ substantially from the rational proportion of individuals facing other kinds of economic trade-offs in earlier studies.19 Of course, GARP violations and the CCEI index may be influenced by the specific combination of budget sets used across different studies, making the comparisons inTable 2hard to interpret. Therefore, we also conducted a follow-up condition in which a separate group of participants recruited from the same population as those in our main privacy experiment made identical choices to those in stages 2 and 3, but with respect to combinations of monetary losses (for details of the design and detailed analysis, see Online Appendix F and Online Appendix G). As in our main experiment, we used an initial set of unrestricted two-dimensional choices to classify the orientation of these participants’ preferences toward money. Not surprisingly, a very large majority (97%) of participants are classified as valuing money, meaning that they selected bundles that minimized their total monetary losses. The bottom row ofTable 2summarizes the degree of rationality observed in the choices made by such money-valuing types in this follow-up experiment. The average CCEI of the types who like money (1.0599) is not statistically different from the CCEI for the private types (1.0425) making privacy trade-offs. Therefore, the perturbation that is required for rationalizing privacy choices is similar when compared with the degree that is necessary to perturb budget sets to eliminate GARP violations when making monetary decisions. This comparison shows that people making trade-offs involving privacy exhibit similar consistency as individuals making monetary trade-offs.20 4.2. Consistency Between Monetary Equivalents and Privacy Choices The second part of our results informs the possibility of efficiently allocating privacy by monetizing personal information. This part extends the study of the rationality of privacy choices to cases involving trade-offs between monetary benefits and privacy protection. We investigate the consistency between both the preference relations and privacy attitudes revealed in stage 1 and their monetary equivalents elicited in stage 2. 4.2.1. Consistency Between Monetary Equivalents and the Privacy Preference Relation. First, we construct a measure of efficiency loss when individuals translate their privacy preferences into monetary valuations. We use the directly revealed preference relation in each decision problem involving a downward-sloping budget set from stage 1—that is, because for each budget line from stage 1, one bundle presented in stage 2 was chosen, and the other (selected by the computer) was not—and compare this relation with the monetary equivalents for the chosen and unchosen options from stage 2. To be specific, in stage 2, we elicit the willingness to accept for the 16 choices selected by a subject from downward-sloping budget sets in stage 1B and for the 16 perturbed choices selected by the computer. Because the former is directly revealed as preferred to the latter, when the monetary equivalent of a perturbed choice is smaller than that of the chosen choice on the same budget line, the difference between the two WTAs signals the size of efficiency loss when people are asked to represent their privacy preference in monetary values. We use the sum of the differences across all 16 budget lines, which we refer to as MVN, as our measure of efficiency loss in the translation of directly elicited privacy preferences to monetary valuations.21 Overall, 46.3% of subjects display perfect consistency (i.e., MVN = 0) when representing their choices from stage 1 in terms of monetary equivalents. However, the remaining 53.7% of subjects exhibit some degree of inconsistency when indicating their privacy preferences in money, and these inconsistent subjects include both subjects classified as rational and irrational based on their stage 1 choices. To be specific, 47% of rational subjects and 65% of irrational subjects show preference reversals when representing their stage 1 preference in monetary terms in stage 2. This result indicates that a significant proportion of subjects exhibit privacy preference reversals when assessing privacy preferences with monetary equivalents, even for subjects who seem to rationally choose privacy levels for different personal information items when monetary trade-offs are not involved. Despite the fact that about half of both the rational and irrational privacy types exhibit noise when representing their preferences in monetary units, there is a stark difference between the size of noise between these two groups.Figure 4shows that the average total MVN (the gray bar) for those subjects classified as irrational is about CHF 16.76, which is more than 2.6 times the size of the average total MVN for rational subjects: CHF 6.38.22Because the standard deviation of MVN is smaller for rational subjects than for irrational subjects, we apply at-test for unequal variance data and find that this difference is significant at the 0.1% level (t= −3.572). Notice that this difference is not mechanical; GARP violations in part 1 do not preclude a subject from having zero MVN in part 2 because the MVN is always computed using the monetary valuations of a chosen bundle and a similar unchosen one. Moreover, it is not driven by subjects whose behavior makes them outliers because the MVN of irrational types is higher than that of rational types for each quartile.23Therefore, the comparisons indicate that trading privacy against money may induce very different monetary consequences for people who do and do not obey GARP in the simple trade-off tasks in stage 1.24 4.2.2. Consistency Between Monetary Equivalents and Privacy Attitudes. Because a significant proportion of subjects have nonzero MVN—implying that WTA may be a noisy measure of privacy values at the individual level—a natural question is whether monetary valuations for sharing personal information capture variation in underlying privacy attitudes, for example, between different groups of people. The answer is important because monetary equivalents are extensively used for accessing the value of privacy. Our results confirm that monetary equivalents can discriminate between different types of privacy attitudes. We compare the average monetary compensation required by private and public types for the four preset bundles that hold fixed, across participants, the combinations of viewers: (20,20), (65,65), (0,65), and (65,0). For these combinations, we have WTA values for identical bundles for every participant in our study. We then calculate for each subject, WTApreset, the average WTA of these four preset combinations, and compare the mean of WTApresetfor private and public subjects, according to the classification in stage 1. Figure 5presents the average WTApresetfor each subject, as well as the quartiles and means for private and public types. It shows that the demanded price for the private types tends to be higher than that for public types at every quartile. The mean WTApreset(the gray line in the box plot) of private subjects (18.61 CHF) is significantly higher than that of public subjects (11.35 CHF,p< 0.05,t= 2.661 in at-test with unequal variance).25Hence, despite the heterogeneity and the noise present in individuals’ privacy choices involving monetary trade-offs, these results suggest that there is value in employing monetary equivalents as a privacy attitude measure, at least for discriminating between groups of individuals with different privacy values. 4.3. Do Laboratory Preferences Correlate with Attitudes and Behaviors? The third part of our analysis investigates whether subjects’ privacy attitudes and their degree of rationality in our laboratory setting exhibit some relationship with subjects’ privacy behavior outside the laboratory. The existence of a correlation provides some support for the notion that our measures can proxy for more general forms of real-life information-sharing behavior. Hence, we cautiously aim here only to conduct an exploratory test of the relationship between our laboratory measures and some real-world behaviors but are cautious not to make too much of any relationships that we observe. 4.3.1. Measures of Daily-Life Information Sharing. To test whether privacy attitudes and measures of rationality in this study correlate with how people behave in more natural privacy decisions, we utilize the incentivized daily-life information-sharing behavior data from stage 3 to construct four variables: (1)Facebook Public: the number of information items a subject shares with all Facebook users on their profile page,26including individuals not on the friend list; (2)Social Media Public: the number of social networking accounts on which a subject posts public content that any user can see;27(3)Customer memberships: the number of categorized customer memberships or club programs a subject uses;28and (4)Location shared: whether the subject’s smartphone has location services enabled.29Individuals who chose to leave one of these questions unanswered are excluded from the analysis in this section, which leaves us 198 out of 216 subjects.30 In our exploratory analysis, we first conduct principal-component factor analysis using the four variables as inputs, with orthogonal Varimax rotation, and obtain two factors of daily-life information-sharing behavior: sharing in social networks and the exchange of personal information for money or services.31We then investigate the extent to which these two factors correlate with our individual-level privacy preference measures. Specifically, we study whether privacy attitudes or the rationality of privacy preferences are predictive for heterogeneity in real-life information sharing at the individual level, controlling for socioeconomic and personal characteristics that have been documented to be influential for personal data-sharing decisions. Table 3presents regression results, using either the measure of sharing in social networks (factor 1) or the exchange of personal information for money and services (factor 2) as the dependent variable. The explanatory variables of interest are indicators for whether a participant is classified as a private type or as an irrational type based on that participant’s choices in stage 1. Model 1 shows that individuals who are public types in our experiment share significantly more personal data in social networks than private types (by 0.91 standard deviation of factor 1). Model 2 confirms that this result is robust after controlling for several socioeconomic and personal characteristics: income, sex, education, and marital status. Models 3 and 4 suggest that rationality has no effect on sharing in social networks. Models 5 and 6 show that privacy attitudes have no impact on the exchange of personal information for money and services. However, Models 7 and 8 provide modest evidence that irrational types tend to trade their personal information for money or services more often than rational types (by 0.25 standard deviations of factor 2), though the results are only marginally statistically significant. Although they are correlational and exploratory, these relationships suggest that attitudes toward and decisions involving privacy measured using simple choice tasks in stage 1 of our experiment may underlie broader behaviors related to privacy. Table 3.Relationships Between Privacy-Preference Measures and Daily-Life Data Sharing Table 3.Relationships Between Privacy-Preference Measures and Daily-Life Data Sharing Notes. Controls include income (categorical), sex (dummy), education (categorical), and marital status (categorical). The two item-dependent types (IQ+ and BF+) are omitted for regressions usingPublicas an independent variable (i.e., Models (1), (2), (5), and (6)). Results are robust when including item-dependent types (coded as either public = 0 or public = 1) in these models; see Online Appendix Table A.4 for more details.t-Statistics are given in parentheses. +p< 0.10; *p< 0.05; ***p< 0.001. 4.3.2. General Privacy Attitude and Privacy Choices. Our final analysis examines the consistency between individuals’ self-reported general privacy attitudes and their behavior in the experiment. People who claim to care about privacy seldom demonstrate comparable concerns in their daily-life data-sharing behavior. This discrepancy between the stated privacy attitudes and behaviors is known as the “privacy paradox” (Athey et al. 2017,Kokolakis 2017). We test if this inconsistency persists even in the very simple setting that we study. Our results indicate a high consistency between the choice-based privacy attitudes measured in stage 1 and the self-reported general privacy attitude, elicited using a widely employed survey question, for both the irrational and the rational types.General Privacy Attitudeis measured by a subject’s response, on a five-point Likert scale, for the question, “In general, how important do you think privacy is?”32Table 4reports ordered-logistic regression results, using theGeneral Privacy Attitudeas the dependent variable; the outputs are proportional odds ratios. Model 1 shows that individuals who are classified as public types based on their stage 1 choices report significantly lower general privacy concerns (z= −4.11) than private types. Model 2 confirms that this result is robust after controlling for socioeconomic and personal characteristics. Therefore, our choice-based classification of privacy attitudes appears to strongly relate to the self-reported attitude frequently used in research and policy. Table 4.Correlation Between Choice-Based Privacy Attitudes and Self-Reported General Privacy Attitudes Table 4.Correlation Between Choice-Based Privacy Attitudes and Self-Reported General Privacy Attitudes Notes. Ordered logistic regression; coefficients are proportional odds ratios. Controls include income (categorical), sex (dummy), education (categorical), and marital status (categorical). The two item-dependent types (IQ+ and BF+) are omitted because we usePublicas an independent variable.z-Statistics are given in parentheses. ***p< 0.001.
article.5. Discussion and Conclusion: This paper provides evidence of people’s ability to rationally manage the sharing of their personal data. We study this by presenting subjects with simple decisions involving trade-offs between sharing different kinds of personal information items and observing the extent to which their choices satisfy basic standards of economic rationality. More precisely, we present a novel choice experiment consisting of three main parts. First, people confront decisions in which they allocate privacy levels between sharing different personal information items. Second, they decide at what prices they are willing to trade control over their personal data for money under various potential uses. At the end of the experiment, we also collect incentivized details of daily-life information sharing and elicit subjects’ general privacy attitudes. Through these three kinds of data, we provide several insights into the rationality and consistency of privacy behavior. In stage 1, we document considerable heterogeneity in both privacy attitudes in the domains in our experiment and in the rationality with which people confront trade-offs in these domains. A majority of individuals in our data want to minimize the information shared, whereas smaller numbers of others desire to share one or both pieces of information. We then measure rationality using a standard paradigm in experimental economics that has been employed in other domains. Despite the heterogeneity of privacy attitudes, most individuals allocate privacy levels across different information domains in a manner that is consistent with the GARP, thereby satisfying basic economic notions of rationality. It is important to note that we study a very simple choice context in which there are only two domains of information sharing, the consequences of sharing public data are simple and known, and we make it easy for people to exert full control over their personal data. The fact that a substantial proportion of subjects exhibit violations of even very simple notions of rationality in this setting suggests that rationality may be much lower as one moves to more complex natural settings. This is a source of concern for extrapolating our results to richer and more complex settings. Our observation that roughly two-thirds of individuals exhibit high levels of rationality presents more of an upper bound than a number likely to generalize to other contexts. Nevertheless, one useful feature of the simple choice context we study is that it allows us to compare the degree of consistency in individual decisions involving privacy with comparable measures involving choices in other contexts. Here, we find that when sharing different kinds of personal information, people are generally similarly consistent with how they behave in other settings (both with comparisons to earlier studies and to monetary choices in our follow-up study). Therefore, personal information sharing does not yield more significant inconsistency when it involves clear trade-offs with known consequences, relative to similar choices involving trade-offs in other domains. Second, there is further inconsistency when translating privacy preferences into monetary values. In stage 2, we find that even among people who satisfy GARP—and therefore may have a consistent underlying preference ordering involving simple privacy trade-offs—47% of individuals exhibit inconsistency when asked to provide valuations for their private information. Thus, even among people who might be classified as rational using a standard economic benchmark, we observe substantial inconsistency and preference reversals when translating their privacy preferences into money. Nevertheless, it is also worth noting that the people who are irrational when allocating privacy levels to different information items forgo a much larger amount of money (2.6 times as much) through their inconsistency than do rational types. These inconsistencies in assigning monetary valuations to one’s private information—even in a highly simple setting with easily understood consequences—cast some doubt on the feasibility of improving the efficiency of privacy policy by monetizing or licensing personal information, at least for a substantial subset of the population. This suggests additional policy interventions may be particularly helpful when monetary valuation and pricing are involved in privacy choices. It is also worth noting that, across the two stages, the percentage of people who are perfectly consistent across these two types of decisions is only 33%. This result suggests that privacy decisions involving more varied and complex choices may lead to additional inconsistencies and irrationality. Given that only one-third of individuals act consistently across even the simple choices with which they are confronted in stages 1 and 2, the proportion likely to do so across all instances of decisions involving privacy that they encounter outside the laboratory is likely to be much lower. Thus, it is very likely that even people who exhibit perfect valuation consistency through stages 1 and 2 may still be highly susceptible to other biases when making privacy-related decisions, particularly when the context involves greater complexity (e.g.,Smith et al. 2011,Adjerid et al. 2018,Xu and Zhang 2022,Tomaino et al. 2023). Finally, we also provide exploratory evidence on the extent to which privacy preferences in different domains are connected. First, monetary valuations, despite the noise at the individual level, nevertheless capture the underlying aggregate privacy preferences of private and public types. Second, individuals who are irrational when deciding privacy levels for different information items squander larger amounts when trading off privacy control for money. Third, laboratory privacy preference measures appear to have some relationship to at least some domains of real-world privacy behaviors elicited using incentivized measures and to general self-reported privacy attitudes measured with a question that is often used in research and policy.33Therefore, our behavioral measures not only provide insights into the internal consistency of privacy preferences in the laboratory but are also potentially informative about behaviors and attitudes outside the laboratory. Nevertheless, the correlations with real-world privacy behaviors are exploratory and should be taken with caution—individuals may share personal information in everyday life for different reasons. Overall, our results provide novel evidence on three fundamental privacy issues: (1) the extent to which rationally managing personal information sharing is inherently more challenging than similar trade-offs in other domains, (2) the feasibility of improving the efficiency of privacy rights through individuals’ monetary valuation of their personal information, and (3) the heterogeneity in privacy attitudes and the degree of rationality of privacy preferences and their implications for information sharing in a variety of contexts encountered in modern life. A better understanding of these issues is critical for the design of effective policies that balance the benefits of information sharing against potential costs from individuals’ irrationality. It is also valuable for understanding how individuals and consumers confront privacy decisions across a variety of settings.
